r convert month year to ordered numeric  i have a dataset with column where the values are month year in format like this ```  m_yr  march 1990  april 1990  may   1990  june  1990  july  1990  aug   1990  sept  1990  oct   1990  nov   1990  dec   1990  jan   1991  feb   1991  march 1991  april 1991  may   1991  june  1991  july  1991  aug   1991  sept  1991  oct   1991  nov   1991  dec   1991 ```  i tried this approach. ``` df$col1 <- as.numeric(df$m_yr) ```  this does converts the `month year` variable to numeric but the order is scrambled and not in right sequence. so i am wondering what is an efficient way to create this numeric variable without writing a lengthy `case_when` statement.  any suggestion is much appreciated. thanks. r convert month year to ordered numeric  i have a dataset with column where the values are month year in format like this ```  m_yr  march 1990  april 1990  may   1990  june  1990  july  1990  aug   1990  sept  1990  oct   1990  nov   1990  dec   1990  jan   1991  feb   1991  march 1991  april 1991  may   1991  june  1991  july  1991  aug   1991  sept  1991  oct   1991  nov   1991  dec   1991 ```  i tried this approach. ``` df$col1 <- as.numeric(df$m_yr) ```  this does converts the `month year` variable to numeric but the order is scrambled and not in right sequence. so i am wondering what is an efficient way to create this numeric variable without writing a lengthy `case_when` statement.  any suggestion is much appreciated. thanks. to convert a "month year" column (like "march 1990", "april 1990" etc.) into an ordered numeric variable in r, you should first parse the date values properly, then transform them into a format that can be meaningfully numeric and ordered (such as the number of months since a reference date). directly using as.numeric() on the string values leads to undesired results, because it just converts factors to their internal codes, not by calendar sequence.

here’s an efficient approach using base r functions:

**1. parse the month-year strings into date objects:**

you can use as.date() together r convert month year to ordered numeric parsing date components in multi-columns is faster with a format in [196]: i = pd.date_range( , periods=10000)

usestate set method not changing my state - react native  i'm working on a part of an application dealing with a deck of cards. for however many cards a player is supposed to have, i am looping through a deck of cards held in a state, randomly picking a card, assigning it to that player and then removing it from the deck. this all works correctly but when the deck is empty (.length === 0), i want to invoke a shuffle() method that will set the deck equal to the discard pile which is also an array held in a usestate. the discard pile is also working as expected but for some reason when trying to set the currentdeck to the discard pile, using setcurrentdeck(discardpile), the currentdeck array remains empty. (i've also tried setcurrentdeck([...discardpile])).  i know this mostly likely has to do with how react handles batching hook calls, and that the value of currentdeck is not what i think it is because of the other setcurrentdeck() calls, but i'm still not able to figure out what's going on.  my code (simplified): ``` const [currentdeck, setcurrentdeck] = usestate(deck) // 'deck' is a hard coded array of cards const [discardpile, setdiscardpile] = usestate([]) const [faceupcard, setfaceupcard] = usestate(null)    useeffect(() => {     if (currentdeck.length === 0) {       const temp = discardpile;       setcurrentdeck([...temp]);       setdiscardpile([]);     }   }, [currentdeck]);    function deal() {     if (readytodeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentdeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);         }       }       const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);       setfaceupcard(currentdeck[rand]);       currentdeck.splice(rand, 1);       setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);       setreadytodeal(false);     }   } ```  i have also tried setting currentdeck with a shuffle method like this: ```   function shuffle() {     const temp = discardpile;     setcurrentdeck([ ...temp]);     setdiscardpile([]);   }    function deal() {     if (readytodeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           if (currentdeck.length === 0) {             shuffle();           }           const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentdeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);         }       }       const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);       setfaceupcard(currentdeck[rand]);       currentdeck.splice(rand, 1);       setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);       setreadytodeal(false);     }   } ```  but still no luck. any help or advice would be appreciated. thank you! usestate set method not changing my state - react native  i'm working on a part of an application dealing with a deck of cards. for however many cards a player is supposed to have, i am looping through a deck of cards held in a state, randomly picking a card, assigning it to that player and then removing it from the deck. this all works correctly but when the deck is empty (.length === 0), i want to invoke a shuffle() method that will set the deck equal to the discard pile which is also an array held in a usestate. the discard pile is also working as expected but for some reason when trying to set the currentdeck to the discard pile, using setcurrentdeck(discardpile), the currentdeck array remains empty. (i've also tried setcurrentdeck([...discardpile])).  i know this mostly likely has to do with how react handles batching hook calls, and that the value of currentdeck is not what i think it is because of the other setcurrentdeck() calls, but i'm still not able to figure out what's going on.  my code (simplified): ``` const [currentdeck, setcurrentdeck] = usestate(deck) // 'deck' is a hard coded array of cards const [discardpile, setdiscardpile] = usestate([]) const [faceupcard, setfaceupcard] = usestate(null)    useeffect(() => {     if (currentdeck.length === 0) {       const temp = discardpile;       setcurrentdeck([...temp]);       setdiscardpile([]);     }   }, [currentdeck]);    function deal() {     if (readytodeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentdeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);         }       }       const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);       setfaceupcard(currentdeck[rand]);       currentdeck.splice(rand, 1);       setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);       setreadytodeal(false);     }   } ```  i have also tried setting currentdeck with a shuffle method like this: ```   function shuffle() {     const temp = discardpile;     setcurrentdeck([ ...temp]);     setdiscardpile([]);   }    function deal() {     if (readytodeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           if (currentdeck.length === 0) {             shuffle();           }           const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentdeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);         }       }       const rand = math.floor(math.random() * currentdeck.length);       setfaceupcard(currentdeck[rand]);       currentdeck.splice(rand, 1);       setcurrentdeck([...currentdeck]);       setreadytodeal(false);     }   } ```  but still no luck. any help or advice would be appreciated. thank you! this is a common issue when working with the react usestate hook, especially when dealing with complex state logic such as updating arrays, shuffling, and reusing values that depend on fresh state. the core of your issue likely stems from misunderstanding how state updates are batched and scheduled in react, and the fact that state updates (via setstate) are asynchronous and not immediately reflected in your variables during a single function’s execution.

### why setcurrentdeck(discardpile) doesn’t behave as expected

when you call setcurrentdeck(discardpile), you schedule a state update, but that update will not take effect until the next render unlike regular javascript variables, react state behaves more like a snapshot. setting it does not change the state variable you already have, but instead triggers a re-render. this can be surprising at first! console.log(count);  // 0  setcount(count + 1); // request a re-render with 1 console.log(count);  // still 0! this behavior help you avoid subtle bugs.

using discrete colors for map fill in mapboxgl instead of default interpoolated colors?  the way i've typically seen mapboxgl fill properties work on choropleth maps is something like this: ``` map.on('load', function () {       map.addsource('bb', { type: 'geojson', data: data, generateid: true});       map.addlayer({         'id': 'berlin',         'type': 'fill',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'fill-color': {           'property': some_numeric_val,           'stops': [[4, '#feebe2'], [8, '#fbb4b9'], [12, '#f768a1'], [16, '#c51b8a'], [20, '#7a0177']]           },         'fill-opacity': .65           }       });       map.addlayer({         'id': 'berlin-stroke',         'type': 'line',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'line-color': '#000',           'line-width': [           'case',             ['boolean', ['feature-state', 'hover'], false],             2,             .5           ]         }       });     }); ```  i.e. the colors are created based on a property that the user selects. however, it seems like mapboxgl's default behavior is to interpolate colors. for example, if one of my geographic units has a value is somewhere between the breakpoints, mapboxgl will interpolate the color, resulting in a gradient of colors.  is there a way to make the colors distinct (non-interpolated)? i.e. if value is 4 or less, the color is #feebe2, if the value is 8 or less, the color is '#fbb4b9', for a total of 5 discrete colors in the example i have here.  i have not been able to find an answer to this anywhere. thanks. using discrete colors for map fill in mapboxgl instead of default interpoolated colors?  the way i've typically seen mapboxgl fill properties work on choropleth maps is something like this: ``` map.on('load', function () {       map.addsource('bb', { type: 'geojson', data: data, generateid: true});       map.addlayer({         'id': 'berlin',         'type': 'fill',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'fill-color': {           'property': some_numeric_val,           'stops': [[4, '#feebe2'], [8, '#fbb4b9'], [12, '#f768a1'], [16, '#c51b8a'], [20, '#7a0177']]           },         'fill-opacity': .65           }       });       map.addlayer({         'id': 'berlin-stroke',         'type': 'line',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'line-color': '#000',           'line-width': [           'case',             ['boolean', ['feature-state', 'hover'], false],             2,             .5           ]         }       });     }); ```  i.e. the colors are created based on a property that the user selects. however, it seems like mapboxgl's default behavior is to interpolate colors. for example, if one of my geographic units has a value is somewhere between the breakpoints, mapboxgl will interpolate the color, resulting in a gradient of colors.  is there a way to make the colors distinct (non-interpolated)? i.e. if value is 4 or less, the color is #feebe2, if the value is 8 or less, the color is '#fbb4b9', for a total of 5 discrete colors in the example i have here.  i have not been able to find an answer to this anywhere. thanks. by default, mapbox gl js uses interpolated color ramps for properties like fill-color in a choropleth map, which means it smoothly transitions between color "stops" based on your data values. this produces a gradient effect where features with values between your specified stops get a color somewhere between those colors. however, if you want to use discrete colors—where each data range is mapped to a single, fixed color with no interpolation—you should use a categorical style expression or a step function rather than stops/interpolate.

how can i list exported values from all of my cloudformation templates?  i'm exporting the name of the stack and the url of my lambda function in my cloudformation template. ``` outputs:   lambdainvokeurl:     value: !getatt myurl.functionurl      export:       name: !sub "${aws::stackname}" ```  i have 8 to 10 stacks exporting similar outputs.  how can i list all exported names & values across all stacks in my aws account?  should i write a new cloudformation template or a lambda function to list them? how can i list exported values from all of my cloudformation templates?  i'm exporting the name of the stack and the url of my lambda function in my cloudformation template. ``` outputs:   lambdainvokeurl:     value: !getatt myurl.functionurl      export:       name: !sub "${aws::stackname}" ```  i have 8 to 10 stacks exporting similar outputs.  how can i list all exported names & values across all stacks in my aws account?  should i write a new cloudformation template or a lambda function to list them? to list all exported values from all of your cloudformation templates (stacks), you do not need to write a new cloudformation template or a lambda function. aws provides a way to list exported outputs directly using the aws management console, aws cli, or aws sdks.

### using aws cli

the quickest method is through the aws command line interface (cli). you can run the following command:

```bash
aws cloudformation list-exports
```

this command returns a list of all exported output names and their values, along with the exporting stack’s name. the output will look like this:

```json
{
  "exports the following ` list-exports  ` example displays a list of the exports from stacks in the current region. aws cloudformation list-exports

is any supertype and also a subtype of all types in typescript?  i am teaching a class on typescript fundamentals and i am trying to really understand the relationships between basic ts types. in all articles i saw, they put the any type very close to the top of the type hierarchy. either  +---------+ | unknown | +---------+      |      v   +-----+   | any |   +-----+      |      v all other types or maybe  +--------------+ | any, unknown | +--------------+      |      v all other types but i do not see it that way. you can assign value of all other types to an unknown variable but you cannot assign unknown value to anything. that puts it on top. but with any, you can assign all values of all types to any and also assign an any value to variables of all types. so it behaves as uknown and never at the same time. from this i would argue, that any stands completely aside of the type hierarchy tree not fitting anywhere in it. i would say any sidesteps the type system completely and in essence means "turn off typechecking"  am i wrong? you are correct in your understanding: the any type in typescript does not neatly fit into the "type hierarchy" the way other types like unknown, never, or primitive types do.

in typescript, any is special. it is sometimes described as both a supertype and a subtype of all types, but that characterization is a bit misleading. what really sets any apart is that it **sidesteps** the type system entirely. when you use any, typescript turns off type checking for that value—you can assign an any value to any other type, and assign any other type to a variable of type any, with no compile-time errors most sharks are cold-blooded. some, like the mako and the great white shark, are partially warm-blooded (they are endotherms). actually, the salmon shark is a warm-blooded shark. great white sharks are some of the only warm-blooded sharks. this allows them to swim in colder waters in addition to warm, tropical waters.

to control an argo workflow (including workflows templated with helm) based on the output of a script (for example, to branch the dag flow), you typically use [when expressions](https://argoproj.github.io/argo-workflows/workflow-conditions/) in argo, not helm's `if` statements. helm flow control (like `{{ if ... }}`) is evaluated at *template render time* (before the workflow runs), whereas control based on script results must occur at *runtime*, after the script step has executed.

certainly! to reverse the byte order (change endianess) in a one-liner on the command line, especially for binary data such as an 8-byte (64-bit) long value written in big endian, you can use `xxd` and `awk` (or `perl`) for flexibility. while tools like `tac` are line-oriented and `dd conv=swab` only swaps 2-byte pairs, you need a tool that will fully reverse the 8-byte sequence. here is a concise and reliable way:

```bash

i use the latest stable language version of c#. passage:

in c# 8.0 and later, interfaces can contain default implementations for members. for example, you can write:

```csharp
interface ithing
{
    string a => "a";
}
```

here, the property `a` has a default implementation, so types that implement `ithing` don’t need to supply their own implementation unless they want to.

regarding the `virtual` modifier: c# does allow you to use the `virtual` keyword on interface members with a body (default implementations), like so:

```csharp
interface ithing
{
    virtual string a => "a the syntax for an interface is relaxed to permit modifiers on its members. the following are permitted: private, protected, internal, public, virtual, abstract, sealed, static, extern, and partial. an interface member whose declaration includes a body is a `virtual` member unless the `sealed` or `private` modifier is used. the `virtual` modifier may be used on a function member that would otherwise be implicitly `virtual`.

when you want to type an object that contains css properties in typescript, you’re right that you shouldn’t try to manually list every possible css property. typescript already provides a built-in interface for this purpose: cssstyledeclaration. however, as you noticed, using partial<cssstyledeclaration> can result in type errors like "type 'string | undefined' is not assignable to type 'string'." this is because the properties on el.style (an instance of cssstyledeclaration) are generally of type string and not string | undefined. when you assign possibly undefined values to them, typescript correctly flags this.

but this mixes fix part of the docker-compose and variable ones.  cheers, olivier when using docker compose with an .env file, you can reference environment variables in your docker-compose.yml file and substitute values like image versions. this works well in the traditional compose workflow, where variables in the .env file (like image_version=1.2.3) are automatically replaced in the compose file (for example: image: myrepo/myimage:${image_version}). this approach makes it easy to upgrade images without editing the compose file directly.

however, when deploying with docker swarm using docker stack deploy, there are some important differences. unlike docker-compose up, docker stack deploy uses a slightly different processing path and only supports variable the most common way to do this is set your stack file with a environment variable for the image tag, which is fed into each docker stack deploy run by your ci/cd system which knows the git commit id, tags the image it just built with that id, pushes it to a registry, and sets it to a envvar during the stack deploy.

**how workflow_dispatch works on non-'master' branches:**

when you create or modify a workflow file (for example, in a feature branch like `branch-a`) and push it to your repository, that workflow is only present in the git history of `branch-a`. the github actions ui tries to surface workflow files based on the currently-selected branch. however, the interface can be a bit confusing:

- the **actions tab** reflects the branch you select, but not always to run a workflow manually, the workflow must be configured to run on the ` workflow_dispatch ` event. to trigger the ` workflow_dispatch ` event, your workflow must be in the default branch. only workflow files that use the ` workflow_dispatch ` event trigger will have the option to run the workflow manually using the **run workflow** button. some events also require the workflow file to be present on the default branch of the repository in order to run.

is there a fiber api in .net?  out of more curiosity than anything i've been looking for a set of c#/.net classes to support fibers/co-routines (the win32 version) and haven't had any luck.  does anybody know of such a beast? is there a fiber api in .net?  out of more curiosity than anything i've been looking for a set of c#/.net classes to support fibers/co-routines (the win32 version) and haven't had any luck.  does anybody know of such a beast? no, .net does not natively offer a direct equivalent to the win32 fibers api. fibers in win32 provide user-mode cooperative multitasking, allowing an application to manually schedule multiple execution contexts (fibers) on a single thread. however, .net abstracts away low-level threading and context switching for portability, safety, and the managed runtime environment, so it does not expose the win32 fiber api directly.

that said, .net provides several mechanisms for concurrency and asynchrony, such as threads, the threadpool, the async/await pattern, and more recently, tasks and the task parallel library (tpl). for coroutine most sharks are cold-blooded. some, like the mako and the great white shark, are partially warm-blooded (they are endotherms). actually, the salmon shark is a warm-blooded shark. great white sharks are some of the only warm-blooded sharks. this allows them to swim in colder waters in addition to warm, tropical waters.

to compare the actual content of two strings, you should use the `.equals()` method. the `.equals()` method checks whether the values within the two strings are equal, regardless of where they are stored in memory. for how do i compare strings in java? i've been using the == operator in my program to compare all my strings so far. however, i ran into a bug, changed one of them into .equals() instead, and it fixed the bug. is == bad? when should it and should it not be used? what's the difference?

a better approach, without adding extra plugins, is to use **event delegation** with jquery's `.on()` method. instead of binding the event directly to each `select` element, you delegate the event to a static parent element ( the .on() method attaches event handlers to the currently selected set of elements in the jquery object. **event handlers are bound only to the currently selected elements; they must exist at the time your code makes the call to .on()**. alternatively, use delegated event handlers to attach event handlers.

**reason:**  
webview2 is essentially an embedded browser, so drag-and-drop events within its content are handled internally by the browser engine (chromium-based) and not by the uwp/xaml environment.

**solution:**  
to disable drag-and you can also let the user drag and drop files into your web application.

java.lang.outofmemoryerror: java heap space  i am getting the following error on execution of a multi-threading program ``` java.lang.outofmemoryerror: java heap space ```  the above error occured in one of the threads.  1. upto my knowledge, heap space is occupied by instance variables only. if this is correct, then why this error occurred after running fine for sometime as space for instance variables are alloted at the time of object creation.  2. is there any way to increase the heap space?  3. what changes should i made to my program so that it will grab less heap space? java.lang.outofmemoryerror: java heap space  i am getting the following error on execution of a multi-threading program ``` java.lang.outofmemoryerror: java heap space ```  the above error occured in one of the threads.  1. upto my knowledge, heap space is occupied by instance variables only. if this is correct, then why this error occurred after running fine for sometime as space for instance variables are alloted at the time of object creation.  2. is there any way to increase the heap space?  3. what changes should i made to my program so that it will grab less heap space? certainly! here is a passage to answer your question:

the error java.lang.outofmemoryerror: java heap space occurs when the java virtual machine (jvm) runs out of memory in the heap area, which is used to allocate objects at runtime.

1. while it is true that the heap space is primarily used for instance variables (i.e., objects created at runtime), the outofmemoryerror can occur after your program runs for some time because your program might continuously allocate new objects without releasing references to old ones. in multi-threaded programs, threads might keep creating new objects or holding onto references, preventing the garbage collector from reclaim heap memory is used by all the parts of the application whereas stack memory is used only by one thread of execution. whenever an object is created, it’s always stored in the heap space and stack memory contains the reference to it. we can use  -xms  and  -xmx  jvm option to define the startup size and maximum size of heap memory.
